content:
First, set up a convenient coordinate system:

– Let \(O=(0,0)\) be the center of the big circle \(C_2\), of radius \(9\).  
– The small circle \(C_1\) of radius \(r\) is tangent to \(C_2\) internally at \(A=(9,0)\).  Hence its center \(I\) lies on the \(x\)–axis at
\[
I=(a,0),\quad r=9-a\,.
\]
– A point \(P=(x,y)\) on \(C_1\) is chosen so that the tangent to \(C_1\) at \(P\) passes through \(O\).  Equivalently, \(OP\perp IP\).  
– The ray \(OP\) meets \(C_2\) again at \(Q\), and the tangent to \(C_1\) at \(A\) (which is the vertical line \(x=9\)) meets the line \(OP\) at \(R\).  
– We are given \(PQ=QR\), and we want \(OP=t\).

---

1.  Equation of the small circle and the right‐angle condition:
\[
\begin{cases}
(x - a)^2 + y^2 = r^2 = (9 - a)^2,\\
OP\perp IP\;\Longrightarrow\; (x,y)\cdot(x - a,y)=0 
\;\Longrightarrow\; x^2 + y^2 = a\,x.
\end{cases}
\]
Set \(t^2 = x^2 + y^2\).  Then \(t^2 = a\,x\), so \(x = t^2/a\).

2.  Plug into the circle‐equation:
\[
(x - a)^2 + y^2 = (9-a)^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\bigl(x^2 + y^2\bigr)\;-\;2a\,x + a^2 =81-18a+a^2
\;\Longrightarrow\;
a\,x -2a\,x =81-18a
\;\Longrightarrow\;
-\,a\,x =81-18a
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x =18 - \frac{81}{a}.
\]
Hence
\[
t^2 = a\,x =18a -81.
\]

3.  Coordinates of \(Q\) and \(R\).  Since \(Q\) lies on the ray \(OP\) with \(\bigl|OQ\bigr|=9\), one finds
\[
Q=\frac{9}{t}\,P,
\quad
\text{so }
PQ=9-t.
\]
The line \(OP\) is parameterized by \(s\mapsto s\,(x,y)/t\).  The vertical line \(x=9\) meets it when
\[
\frac{s\,x}{t}=9\quad\Longrightarrow\quad s=\frac{9t}{x},
\]
so \(R\) is at distance \(OR=\tfrac{9t}{x}\).  Thus
\[
QR=OR-OQ=\frac{9t}{x}-9=9\Bigl(\frac{t}{x}-1\Bigr).
\]
The condition \(PQ=QR\) is
\[
9-t \;=\;9\Bigl(\frac{t}{x}-1\Bigr)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
18 - t = \frac{9t}{x}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x\,(18-t)=9t.
\]
But \(x=t^2/a\), so
\[
\frac{t^2}{a}\,(18-t)=9t
\;\Longrightarrow\;
18t-t^2=9a
\;\Longrightarrow\;
t^2-18t+9a=0.
\]
Combined with \(t^2=18a-81\), one solves for \(a\) and finds \(a=5\) (the other root \(a=9\) makes the small circle degenerate).  Then
\[
t^2=18\cdot5-81=9
\;\Longrightarrow\;
t=3.
\]

Therefore
\[
\boxed{OP=3}.
\]

First, set up a convenient coordinate system:

– Let \(O=(0,0)\) be the center of the big circle \(C_2\), of radius \(9\).  
– The small circle \(C_1\) of radius \(r\) is tangent to \(C_2\) internally at \(A=(9,0)\).  Hence its center \(I\) lies on the \(x\)–axis at
\[

– A point \(P=(x,y)\) on \(C_1\) is chosen so that the tangent to \(C_1\) at \(P\) passes through \(O\).  Equivalently, \(OP\perp IP\).  
– The ray \(OP\) meets \(C_2\) again at \(Q\), and the tangent to \(C_1\) at \(A\) (which is the vertical line \(x=9\)) meets the line \(OP\) at \(R\).  
– We are given \(PQ=QR\), and we want \(OP=t\).

\;\Longrightarrow\;

\[

\]

\quad

\[
\frac{s\,x}{t}=9\quad\Longrightarrow\quad s=\frac{9t}{x},
\]

\[
QR=OR-OQ=\frac{9t}{x}-9=9\Bigl(\frac{t}{x}-1\Bigr).

\[
9-t \;=\;9\Bigl(\frac{t}{x}-1\Bigr)
\;\Longrightarrow\;
18 - t = \frac{9t}{x}
\;\Longrightarrow\;
x\,(18-t)=9t.
\]

\[
\frac{t^2}{a}\,(18-t)=9t
\;\Longrightarrow\;
18t-t^2=9a
\;\Longrightarrow\;
t^2-18t+9a=0.

\[
t^2=18\cdot5-81=9
\;\Longrightarrow\;
t=3.
\]

\[
\boxed{OP=3}.
\]
